title: How Cincinnati Museum Center Built an AI Learning Community
content:
Most museum staff don’t need to be convinced that AI is changing things. What they need is somewhere safe to figure out what that actually means for their work — and permission to be honest about how they feel. Cincinnati Museum Center decided to build that space themselves.

The museum — a natural history and science museum, a history museum, and a children’s museum, all housed within a National Historic Landmark art deco train station — has a staff of around 180. When Whitney Owens, Chief Learning Officer, and her colleagues began thinking about how to approach AI, they didn’t start with a policy or a training programme. They started with a question: how do you bring people who feel completely differently about something into the same room and get them to learn together?

At the Museum AI Summit 2026, Owens was joined by Jason Perkins, Vice President of Technology, and Katie Webb, Senior Director of Digital Assets Management, to share what they tried and what they found.
snippet: Most museum staff don’t need to be convinced that AI is changing things. What they need is somewhere safe to figure out what that actually means for their work — and permission to be honest about how
creator: Manual Charr

title: How a Museum in Rural Wyoming Reaches Students on Six Continents
content:
The Buffalo Bill Center of the West sits in Cody, Wyoming — a small city in the high desert, miles from any major population centre, encircled by mountains and cut off from much of the country by winter.

It is not, in the obvious sense, an easy place to reach. And yet, since 2013, the museum’s virtual field trip programme has connected more than 275,000 students to its galleries, collections, and educators — from classrooms on the East Coast of America to schools in Mexico, Canada, and beyond.

At the Museums & Schools Summit 2026, the Center’s education team shared how that programme started, how it grew, and what it actually looks like in practice — for the staff delivering it as much as the students receiving it.

When the Centre’s education team first began thinking about virtual outreach, the problem was straightforward: geography. Around 5,000 students a year can make it into the building in person — a modest figure for a museum with five distinct collections, but one that reflects the reality of rural Wyoming. Distances are long. Winters are harsh. School trips are complicated and expensive.
snippet: The Buffalo Bill Center of the West sits in Cody, Wyoming — a small city in the high desert, miles from any major population centre, encircled by mountains and cut off from much of the country by
creator: Manual Charr

title: From Field Trip to Career Path: The Taubman Museum’s School Partnerships
content:
Most museums measure their relationship with schools in hours. A visit, a tour, a workshop — then the bus pulls away. The Taubman Museum of Art in Roanoke, Virginia, has been trying something far more ambitious: a sustained, four-year programme that brings high school students into the museum not as visitors, but as participants.

The Museum Studies programme at the Taubman has been running since 2009, and it has grown considerably since then. What began with seven students in a single senior class now attracts roughly a hundred applicants each year competing for 25 available spots. The waitlist, in its own way, tells you something important: there is genuine appetite among young people for this kind of experience, when it is offered.

At the Museums & Schools Summit 2026, Katrina King-Singh, Deputy Director of Community Engagement at the Taubman, joined art teacher Natalie Strum and museum educator Mary Novick to explain how the programme works, why it works, and what it has meant — for students, for the museum, and for the broader community.
snippet: Most museums measure their relationship with schools in hours. A visit, a tour, a workshop — then the bus pulls away. The Taubman Museum of Art in Roanoke, Virginia, has been trying something far more
creator: Manual Charr

title: How a Museum Put History on the School Menu
content:
What if the most effective way to get students engaging with history wasn’t a lesson, a worksheet, or even a museum visit — but lunch? At the Museums & Schools Summit 2026, Claire Géraud-Stewart and Quentin Arné from the Musées départementaux du Val d’Oise presented a project that has been doing exactly that for the past seven years.

The archeo meals programme is, on the surface, a simple idea. Twice a year, every public high school in the Val d’Oise département — a largely urban area of 1.2 million people just northwest of Paris — serves its students a historically inspired lunch. In spring, the menu draws on medieval food traditions. In autumn, it’s Roman. Students eat dishes like stir-fried pork with dried fruit, or stuffed dates filled with almonds, pine nuts, apricots, and honey — all prepared by the authority’s own school chefs and cleared for historical authenticity by museum staff.

The reality, though, is more ambitious than it first appears.
snippet: What if the most effective way to get students engaging with history wasn’t a lesson, a worksheet, or even a museum visit — but lunch? At the Museums & Schools Summit 2026, Claire Géraud-Stewart and
creator: Manual Charr

title: Who Gets to Tell the Story? Co-Producing Digital Meaning in Museums
content:
How the Great North Museum: Hancock and Beacon Films are reshaping what museum interpretation can look like — and who gets to make it.

What happens when you stop asking disabled and neurodivergent people to simply visit your museum, and start inviting them to interpret it?

That question sits at the heart of an ongoing collaboration between the Great North Museum: Hancock in Newcastle and Beacon Films, a production company supporting disabled and neurodivergent filmmakers. Presented at the Museum Digital Summit 2026, the session offered a candid, inspiring look at a partnership that is quietly changing how one museum thinks about stories, spaces and creative authority.

The Great North Museum: Hancock is nearly 150 years old. Its collections span archaeology, natural history and world cultures. It is, in short, full of stories — most of them told by the institution itself.
snippet: How the Great North Museum: Hancock and Beacon Films are reshaping what museum interpretation can look like — and who gets to make it.
creator: Manual Charr

title: Beyond Sustainability: How IMMA Is Learning to Think Like a Living System
content:
What if the goal wasn’t to reduce a museum’s impact, but to actively improve the conditions for life? At the Irish Museum of Modern Art, a new framework called REGEN is asking that question — and beginning to answer it.

For many cultural institutions, sustainability means one thing: doing less harm. Fewer emissions, less waste, more responsible procurement. The logic is one of reduction, and it matters. But Lisa Fitzsimmons, Strategy and Sustainability Lead at the Irish Museum of Modern Art (IMMA), has been asking whether reduction is enough — and whether the museums sector might be ready for something more ambitious.

The framework she has developed in response, REGEN IMMA, starts not with targets but with a shift in how the museum understands itself. “What happens,” she asked in her presentation at MuseumNext, “when you stop thinking about a museum as a building with a carbon footprint and start thinking about it as something more alive — a living organism within a wider ecosystem?”
snippet: What if the goal wasn’t to reduce a museum’s impact, but to actively improve the conditions for life? At the Irish Museum of Modern Art, a new framework called REGEN is asking that question — and
creator: Manual Charr

title: How the Guggenheim Museum Bilbao Made Sustainability Everyone’s Job
content:
Through a transdisciplinary internal team called GU-Zero, the Guggenheim Museum Bilbao has turned environmental action from a specialist function into a shared institutional practice — with measurable results.

When sustainability sits in a single department, it tends to stay there. Decisions made elsewhere in the institution — about how exhibitions are designed, how artworks are shipped, what’s on the menu at a private view — are rarely shaped by environmental thinking, because the people making them aren’t part of the conversation. The Guggenheim Museum Bilbao has spent more than two decades working against that tendency, and the structure it has developed to do so is worth examining in some detail.
snippet: Through a transdisciplinary internal team called GU-Zero, the Guggenheim Museum Bilbao has turned environmental action from a specialist function into a shared institutional practice — with measurable
creator: Manual Charr

title: When Sculptures Speak: How AI Gave a Voice to the Gardens of Versailles
content:
A partnership between Ask Mona, the Palace of Versailles, and OpenAI has turned a walk through one of the world’s most visited gardens into a real-time conversation — letting visitors speak directly with the statues that line its paths.

Imagine walking through the gardens of the Palace of Versailles on a sunny afternoon, stopping in front of a marble figure you don’t recognise, and simply asking who it is. Now imagine the statue answering — in its own voice, with its own personality, ready to discuss its origins, its creator, its place in the mythology of the palace.

That is exactly what Ask Mona co-founder and CEO Marion Carré presented at the Museum Digital Summit 2026: an experiment in conversational AI interpretation that has already generated more than 100,000 visitor messages in its first six months.
snippet: A partnership between Ask Mona, the Palace of Versailles, and OpenAI has turned a walk through one of the world’s most visited gardens into a real-time conversation — letting visitors speak directly
creator: Manual Charr

title: What Happens When You Set an Artwork Free? The Sainsbury Centre’s Day Release Project
content:
What if a museum’s most powerful act wasn’t bringing people through its doors, but sending its artworks out into the world instead? At the Museum Digital Summit 2026, Beatrice Prutton, Digital Communications and Creative Curator at the Sainsbury Centre, presented Day Release — a film series that does exactly that.

Museums have been producing digital content about their collections for years. Explainer videos, curator interviews, virtual tours — the formats are familiar. Day Release is something else entirely. Rather than documenting art within the gallery, the Sainsbury Centre physically transports priceless works out of the museum and into the places that shaped them, then films what happens when they arrive.

The result is a series of scripted, dramatic short films in which artworks appear not as objects to be explained, but as living characters navigating the world — and the people in it.
snippet: What if a museum’s most powerful act wasn’t bringing people through its doors, but sending its artworks out into the world instead? At the Museum Digital Summit 2026, Beatrice Prutton, Digital
creator: Manual Charr

title: How Reykjavík Art Museum Gave 600 Teenagers a Voice on Climate Change
content:
By combining a revived community programme with time-based art and a carefully designed welcome, the Reykjavík Art Museum found a way to reach the teenagers who weren’t coming — and discovered that trust, comfort, and a little fun go a long way.

Museums have long wrestled with the question of how to connect with teenagers. The Reykjavík Art Museum had a more specific version of that problem: of the roughly 9,000 students visiting each year — a significant number in a country of fewer than 400,000 people — only a small proportion were teenagers. And most of those who did come were secondary school art students: young people who already felt at ease in a museum setting.

“We rarely ask how young people experience museums,” says Ariana Katrín Katrínardóttir, the museum’s Education and Mediation Manager. “Do they feel welcome? Do they feel seen? Do they feel trusted?”

Those questions shaped a summer project that brought 600 teenagers into the museum to explore climate issues through contemporary art — and in doing so, offered some practical lessons about what sustainable museum education can look like in practice.
snippet: By combining a revived community programme with time-based art and a carefully designed welcome, the Reykjavík Art Museum found a way to reach the teenagers who weren’t coming — and discovered that
creator: Manual Charr
